```{python}
        "Predicted doublet": "#f59e0b",
        "Removed by doublet filter": "#dc2626",
    }
    n_samples = plot_df_display["sample_display"].nunique()
    g = sns.FacetGrid(
        plot_df_display,
        col="sample_display",
        col_wrap=2 if n_samples <= 4 else 3,
        hue="doublet_filter_status",
        palette=color_map,
        height=3.6,
        aspect=1.35,
        sharex=False,
        sharey=True,
    )
    g.map_dataframe(sns.scatterplot, x="total_counts", y="doublet_score", s=5, linewidth=0, alpha=0.55)
    g.add_legend(title="Doublet filter status")
    g.figure.suptitle("Doublet removal candidates by sample", y=1.03)
    g.set_titles(col_template="{col_name}")
    g.set_axis_labels("Total counts", "Doublet score")
    for ax in g.axes.flat:
        ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MaxNLocator(nbins=4))
        ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(format_count_tick))
    save_static_png(g.figure, "doublet_removal_candidates_by_sample.png")
```

### Cell-Level QC Before Vs After

These interactive figures compare the key QC metrics before and after filtering.
The goal is to make the filtering effect easy to interpret at a glance:

- histograms show how much of each distribution was trimmed
- violin plots show whether the retained object still has sensible spread
- the summary table below can be used directly in methods or supplementary notes

```{python}
compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "Total counts")
```

```{python}
compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "n_genes_by_counts", "Genes detected per cell")
```

```{python}
compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "pct_counts_mt", "Mitochondrial percentage")
```

```{python}
compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "pct_counts_ribo", "Ribosomal percentage")
```

```{python}
compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "pct_counts_hb", "Hemoglobin percentage")
```

```{python}
if pd.to_numeric(long_qc_df["doublet_score"], errors="coerce").notna().any():
    compare_metric_figure(long_qc_df, "doublet_score", "Doublet score")
```

```{python}
format_metric_stage_table(before_after_stats)
```

### Confounder Diagnostics

These panels show how the main QC covariates relate to one another before and
after filtering. They are useful for detecting technical structure that may not
be obvious from one-dimensional distributions alone, such as low-complexity
cells with high mitochondrial load or high-count cells that are enriched for
doublet calls. The default view is sample-focused; condition-specific views are
shown separately only when the condition annotation is informative.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Sample

```{python}
static_confounder_scatter(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "n_genes_by_counts", "sample_display", "Counts vs genes by sample", "confounder_counts_vs_genes_by_sample.png")
```

```{python}
static_confounder_scatter(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "pct_counts_mt", "sample_display", "Counts vs mitochondrial percentage by sample", "confounder_counts_vs_mt_by_sample.png")
```

```{python}
if pd.to_numeric(long_qc_df["doublet_score"], errors="coerce").notna().any():
    static_confounder_scatter(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "doublet_score", "sample_display", "Counts vs doublet score by sample", "confounder_counts_vs_doublet_by_sample.png")
```

#### Condition

```{python}
if condition_available:
    static_confounder_scatter(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "n_genes_by_counts", condition_key, f"Counts vs genes by {condition_key}", "confounder_counts_vs_genes_by_condition.png")
else:
    print(f"No condition-specific diagnostics shown because '{condition_key}' is not defined with at least two non-missing values.")
```

```{python}
if condition_available:
    static_confounder_scatter(long_qc_df, "total_counts", "pct_counts_mt", condition_key, f"Counts vs mitochondrial percentage by {condition_key}", "confounder_counts_vs_mt_by_condition.png")
```

:::

### Sample-Level QC Before Vs After

These summaries make it easier to spot problematic samples, not just problematic
cells. In mixed or clinically heterogeneous cohorts, this section is often the
most important QC review panel because it reveals whether one sample is driving
most exclusions or carrying systematically different metric profiles.

```{python}
format_table(sample_overview_display_df)
```

```{python}
format_table(sample_metric_shift_display_df)
```

```{python}
plot_df = sample_compare_df.sort_values("retained_fraction_after", ascending=False)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=sample_barplot_figsize(len(plot_df)))
sns.barplot(data=plot_df, x="sample_display", y="retained_fraction_after", color="#0f766e", ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Cells retained")
ax.set_xlabel("")
ax.set_ylabel("Cells retained")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda value, _: f"{value:.0%}"))
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)
for label in ax.get_xticklabels():
    label.set_horizontalalignment("right")
fig.tight_layout()
sns.despine(fig=fig)
save_static_png(fig, "retained_cell_fraction_by_sample.png")
```

```{python}
static_sample_metric(sample_compare_long, "median_total_counts_stage", "Median counts", "Median counts", "sample_median_total_counts_before_after.png")
```

```{python}
static_sample_metric(sample_compare_long, "median_n_genes_stage", "Median genes", "Median genes", "sample_median_genes_before_after.png")
```

```{python}
static_sample_metric(sample_compare_long, "median_pct_counts_mt_stage", "Median MT %", "Median MT (%)", "sample_median_mt_before_after.png")
```

## Embeddings And Clustering

These summaries show the retained object after normalization, PCA, graph
construction, UMAP, and Leiden clustering. Review them together rather than in
isolation:

- `by leiden` checks whether the selected clustering granularity is plausible
- `by batch` checks for residual batch structure
- `by condition` checks whether biological structure is interpretable rather than purely technical

### Embeddings

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### PCA

```{python}
static_embedding_plot(filtered, "X_pca", "leiden", "PCA colored by Leiden cluster", "pca_by_leiden.png")
```

```{python}
static_embedding_plot(filtered, "X_pca", batch_key, f"PCA colored by {batch_key}", "pca_by_batch.png")
```

```{python}
if condition_available:
    static_embedding_plot(filtered, "X_pca", condition_key, f"PCA colored by {condition_key}", "pca_by_condition.png")
```

#### Cluster Counts

```{python}
format_table(cluster_counts)
```

:::

### Resolution Diagnostics

The final Leiden resolution is set either manually or by the template heuristic,
but this section benchmarks a grid of alternatives on the same neighbor graph.
Use these panels as a decision aid rather than a strict optimizer: a useful
resolution should be reasonably stable, avoid many tiny clusters, and remain
biologically interpretable in the downstream annotation step.

Interpretation:

- higher modularity can indicate cleaner graph partitioning
- lower Davies-Bouldin score can indicate better separation in PCA space
- higher adjacent ARI/NMI means the clustering changed less from the previous resolution
- small-cluster burden flags over-fragmentation; very small clusters may still be valid, but they need biological or technical justification
- the resolution tree shows whether clusters split gradually or abruptly across the grid

Selected resolution rationale:

```{python}
display(Markdown(leiden_resolution_note))
```

```{python}
static_resolution_diagnostics_plot(resolution_diagnostics_df, "resolution_diagnostics.png")
```

```{python}
static_resolution_selection_plot(resolution_diagnostics_df, "resolution_selection_metrics.png")
```

```{python}
static_resolution_tree_plot(resolution_label_map, resolution_diagnostics_df, "resolution_tree.png")
```

```{python}
format_resolution_table(resolution_diagnostics_df)
```

### Cluster Composition

These composition summaries show whether the inferred clusters are broadly
represented across samples and conditions or dominated by only one subset. This
is especially helpful before downstream differential analysis because it makes
replication and imbalance issues visible early.

```{python}
format_table(cluster_by_sample_counts.reset_index(names="sample_display"))
```

```{python}
static_composition_heatmap(cluster_by_sample_fraction.fillna(0), "Cluster composition by sample (row fractions)", "cluster_composition_by_sample.png")
```

```{python}
if condition_available:
    format_table(cluster_by_condition_counts.reset_index(names=condition_key))
else:
    print(f"No condition-level cluster composition shown because '{condition_key}' is not defined with at least two non-missing values.")
```

```{python}
if condition_available:
    static_composition_heatmap(cluster_by_condition_fraction.fillna(0), f"Cluster composition by {condition_key} (row fractions)", "cluster_composition_by_condition.png")
```

### Final Selected Leiden UMAP

This panel shows the retained object colored by the Leiden clustering actually
used downstream together with the batch labels on the same final UMAP. It is
placed after the resolution review so the chosen clustering and residual batch
structure can be checked directly in their final form.

```{python}
display(
    Markdown(
        f"Final clustering shown here uses Leiden resolution `{resolved_leiden_resolution:.2f}` "
        f"({leiden_resolution_source.replace('_', ' ')})."
    )
)
```

```{python}
embedding_plot(
    filtered,
    "X_umap",
    "leiden",
    f"Final UMAP colored by Leiden cluster at resolution {resolved_leiden_resolution:.2f}",
)
```

```{python}
embedding_plot(
    filtered,
    "X_umap",
    batch_key,
    f"Final UMAP colored by {batch_key} at resolution {resolved_leiden_resolution:.2f}",
)
```

## Appendix

### Methods Parameters

This appendix records the parameter values used in the run. It is designed to
support reproducibility and methods write-up rather than interpretation of the
biological signal itself.

```{python}
format_table(
    pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "parameter": [
                "input_path",
                "input_format",
                "input_source_template",
                "ambient_correction_applied",
                "ambient_correction_method",
                "var_names",
                "organism",
                "sample_id_key",
                "batch_key",
                "condition_key",
                "doublet_method",
                "filter_predicted_doublets",
                "qc_mode",
                "qc_nmads",
                "min_genes",
                "min_cells",
                "min_counts",
                "max_pct_counts_mt",
                "max_pct_counts_ribo",
                "max_pct_counts_hb",
                "n_top_hvgs",
                "n_pcs",
                "n_neighbors",
                "random_state",
                "umap_min_dist",
                "umap_spread",
                "resolution_grid",
                "leiden_resolution",
                "leiden_resolution_source",
            ],
            "value": [
                str(input_path),
                resolved_input_format,
                input_source_template,
                ambient_correction_applied,
                ambient_correction_method,
                var_names,
                organism,
                sample_id_key,
                batch_key,
                condition_key,
                doublet_method,
                filter_predicted_doublets,
                qc_mode,
                qc_nmads,
                min_genes,
                min_cells,
                min_counts,
                max_pct_counts_mt,
                max_pct_counts_ribo,
                max_pct_counts_hb,
                int(cfg["analysis"]["n_top_hvgs"]),
                int(cfg["analysis"]["n_pcs"]),
                int(cfg["analysis"]["n_neighbors"]),
                random_state,
                umap_min_dist,
                umap_spread,
                str(cfg["analysis"].get("resolution_grid", "")),
                resolved_leiden_resolution,
                leiden_resolution_source,
            ],
        }
    )
)
```

### Session Info

This section records the execution environment used to render the report and
write the processed object. It is the quickest way to diagnose version-related
differences between runs.

```{python}
format_table(session_info_df)
```

### References

The workflow in this notebook is based on the Scanpy/scverse ecosystem and is
rendered with Quarto using the Jupyter engine. The following resources are
useful for methodological background and format details:

- Scanpy documentation: <https://scanpy.readthedocs.io/>
- AnnData documentation: <https://anndata.readthedocs.io/>
- Scrublet publication: Wolock SL, Lopez R, Klein AM. Cell Systems (2019). <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cels.2018.11.005>
- Quarto with Jupyter kernels: <https://quarto.org/docs/advanced/jupyter/kernel-execution.html>
- Quarto authoring with Jupyter: <https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/authoring/jupyter.html>
- 10x Genomics Cell Ranger HDF5 matrices: <https://www.10xgenomics.com/support/software/cell-ranger/latest/tutorials/outputs/cr-outputs-h5-matrices>
- Parse Biosciences split-pool workflow documentation: <https://support.parsebiosciences.com/>
- Scale Bio RNA output documentation: <https://github.com/ScaleBio/ScaleRna/blob/master/docs/outputs.md>
